_note: Curated bank/strategist house views for the Forecast Consensus panel. These outlooks are narrative PDFs/HTML with NO data feed, so figures are transcribed by hand from the published report and dated. NEVER invent numbers — only transcribe what the report states, and set as_of to the report's publication month. macro_forecasts.load_house_views() reads the 'forecasters' array and forces live=false. Ranges (fed-funds target range, S&P target range) are stored as the midpoint with the exact published range preserved in 'notes', because the UI formats values as single numbers.

_schema.name: Display name (e.g. JPMorgan WM)
_schema.notes: any caveats (e.g. inflation = CPI; S&P target is year-end)

forecasters.name: Wells Fargo Inv. Institute
forecasters.notes: Released Jun 16, 2026. Inflation = CPI. Fed funds = year-end target range 3.50–3.75% for both 2026 and 2027 (midpoint shown). S&P 500 = year-end target range (midpoint shown): 7,800–8,000 (2026), 8,600–8,800 (2027).

forecasters.name: Fannie Mae (ESR)
forecasters.source: Fannie Mae ESR Economic Forecast: June 2026
forecasters.notes: June 10, 2026 ESR forecast (rates as of May 29, 2026). GDP & CPI are Q4/Q4; unemployment & fed funds are annual averages. Inflation = headline CPI (3.6% 2026 / 1.9% 2027); ESR also publishes core CPI 3.0/2.5, PCE 3.6/2.0, core PCE 3.3/2.2. Fed funds flat at 3.6% (annual avg) through 2027.

forecasters.name: KPMG Economics
forecasters.notes: GDP is Q4/Q4 basis (2.2% 2026, 2.1% 2027). Inflation = PCE (the Fed's gauge), expected to plateau at ~4% later in 2026 (no 2027 point published). KPMG expects the Fed to hike in H2 2026 (no explicit rate level given); unemployment seen in the low-4% range.

forecasters.name: Guggenheim Investments
forecasters.source: Economic Outlook & Key Themes (Monthly Commentary, Jun 2026)
forecasters.notes: GDP = 'real GDP growth holding around 2 percent in 2026' (qualitative point). Fed funds = current target range 3.50–3.75% (midpoint shown), expected held through mid-2027 with rising rate-hike odds. Core PCE seen ending 2026 above 3% (no point figure published); unemployment expected to remain near current levels.

forecasters.name: UBS CIO GWM
forecasters.notes: Forecast table as of Nov 12, 2025; S&P 500 year-end target 7,700 reaffirmed Jan 13, 2026. GDP = annual average. Inflation table labeled simply 'Inflation (%)' — treated as CPI (not explicitly labeled in the doc). Fed funds = year-end (Dec-26) policy rate (3.33%). UBS did not publish an unemployment forecast, nor 2027 S&P/fed-funds figures, in this report.

forecasters.name: Goldman Sachs Research
forecasters.notes: S&P 500 year-end 2026 target 8,000 (raised from 7,600; as of May 26, 2026). Unemployment 4.4% by end-2026 (Jun 9, 2026 update). Core PCE seen 3%+ through 2026 and ~2% in 2027 (ranges, no point). GDP for 2026 (2.8% full-year / 2.5% Q4/Q4) is from the older Jan-2026 vintage so it is not shown here. No 2026 rate cuts expected (policy held at 3.50–3.75%); first cuts seen in 2027 toward a 3–3.25% terminal.

forecasters.name: Morgan Stanley Research
forecasters.notes: S&P 500 year-end 2026 target 8,000 (raised from 7,800; May 15, 2026). MS's detailed US GDP/inflation/unemployment figures are in gated research; the public midyear articles publish only global GDP (3.2% 2026 / 3.4% 2027) and a 2027 Fed terminal of 3–3.25%. The 8,300 figure is a mid-2027 (12-month-forward) S&P level, not a year-end 2027 target, so it is omitted.

forecasters.name: Yardeni Research
forecasters.notes: Yardeni publishes a year-end 2026 S&P 500 RANGE of 6,750–8,250 (forward P/E 18.0–22.0); the midpoint (7,500) is shown here, with 8,250 the top/raised target. No GDP/inflation/unemployment/fed-funds point forecasts in this note.

forecasters.name: Vanguard (VEMO)
forecasters.notes: GDP 2.25% is Q4/Q4 (Dec-10-2025 VEMO baseline, still standing at the Jun-17-2026 update). The mid-2026 update revised core PCE inflation above 3% (from 2.6%), unemployment to the mid-4% range (from 4.2%), and removed the 2026 rate cut (policy rate now seen in its current range through 2027) — those revisions are qualitative, so no updated point figures are shown.
